questions.question: Explain the LR parsing algorithm with its components. Describe the role of the ACTION and GOTO tables.
keyPoints: LR parser has three components: stack, input buffer, and parsing table
ACTION[s, a] determines what to do when in state s with input symbol a
ACTION entries: Shift (push symbol and state), Reduce (pop and push non-terminal), Accept, Error
GOTO[s, A] determines the state to transition to after reducing to non-terminal A
The parser repeats until it accepts or encounters an error
LR parsing produces a rightmost derivation in reverse (bottom-up)

questions.question: Define LR(0) items. Explain the CLOSURE and GOTO operations with examples.
keyPoints: An LR(0) item is a production with a dot indicating parsing progress, e.g., A -> X.YZ
CLOSURE(I): If [A -> alpha.B beta] is in I, add [B -> .gamma] for all productions B -> gamma
GOTO(I, X): Find all items [A -> alpha.X beta] in I, move dot past X, return CLOSURE of result
CLOSURE adds items for non-terminals after the dot; GOTO computes state transitions
Example: For S -> .E, if E -> .E+T | .T, CLOSURE adds these items to the state

questions.question: Differentiate between shift-reduce conflict and reduce-reduce conflict in LR parsing.
keyPoints: Shift-reduce: Parser cannot decide whether to shift input or reduce stack contents for the same lookahead
Reduce-reduce: Parser has two or more valid reductions possible for the same lookahead
Both indicate the grammar cannot be parsed by that particular type of LR parser
Can sometimes be resolved by using a more powerful parser (SLR -> LALR -> CLR)

questions.question: Compare SLR, LALR, and CLR parsers in terms of power and practical usage.
keyPoints: SLR uses FOLLOW sets for conflict resolution, smallest tables, least powerful
LALR merges states with same core, moderate tables, used by YACC/Bison
CLR uses explicit lookahead per item, largest tables, most powerful
Power hierarchy: LR(0) < SLR(1) < LALR(1) < CLR(1)
LALR is the most commonly used in practice as it balances power and table size
